title: practical english usage - Part 296
content:
not how  long something takes. Compare: The  expression in .. .'s time is used to say how  soon something will happen, I can run 200 metres in about 30 seconds.

Ask me again in three or four days.

long something takes to happen.

In can also be used to say how  soon something will happen, and to say how 6 other     uses    of  in Our  house was built in the 15th century. He died in 1616.

I was born in March. Kent is beautiful in spring.

It happened in the week after Christmas.

5 longer     periods:     in What did you do at the weekend?

British people say at the weekend; Americans use on.

What are you doing on Easter Monday?

Come and see us on Christmas Day.

But we  use on to talk about one day of the holiday.

vocabulary •  389   (a)wake and (a)waken I slowly awoke to the danger that threatened me.              + talk about the waking of emotions, understanding, etc.

Awake and awaken are also rather literary words. They  are most often used to Then the prince wakened her with a kiss.

The princess did not waken for a hundred years.

Waken is a more literary alternative to wake (up).

Could you wake me (up) at half past six?

Wake up! It's time to go to work, (     Wake!...) I woke up three times in the night.

it means ‘ stop sleeping'.

'make (somebody else) stop sleeping'. It is often followed by up, especially when Wake is the most common of these four verbs. It can mean 'stop sleeping’  or 1  use 389 (a)  wake      and   (a)waken ►    465 at  last,    finally,      in   the    end    and   at  the    end For first(ly) as a discourse marker, ►    3()1.1.

Note that at last is not the opposite of at first, ►    465.

situation today; and then we'll discuss possible solutions, (     At first, I want First, I want to talk about the history of the problem; then I'll outline the not marriage.) (     ... -when we were at-first-married?) We lived there when we were first married. (= ... in the early days of our not That's mine -1 saw it first! (     .. .4-saw-it at-first?) In other cases, we  usually prefer first.

The work was hard at first, but I got used to it.

At first they were very happy, but then things started going wrong.

by but.

something different that happens/happened later. At first... is often followed We use at first to talk about the beginning of a situation, to make a contrast with 388 at   first     and   first answer to Thank you (►  329.19) and to Do you mind if...? (►  528).

The  expression Not at all is used (especially in British English) as a rather formal 3 Not   at   all.

You can come whenever you like - any time at all.

He'll come before supper if he comes at all.

Do you play poker at all? (= ... even a little?) ever and any.

At all can also be used in questions, and with 'non-affirmative' words like if, 2 questions,       etc Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary  •  390   back (adverb) and again again to suggest ‘ return to an earlier situation’ , etc.

With adverb particles and prepositional phrases, we  can use both back and 4 adverb      particles,      etc Who opened the window? Could you close it again, please?

Stefan can never return to his country again.

an earlier situation'.

However, again can be used to emphasise the idea of ‘ return to not Who opened the window? Could you close it, please? (     .. . close it back...) return back...) Stefan can never return to his country. (More natural than Stefan can never or ‘ movement in the opposite direction’ , back is not generally used.

When  the verb itself already expresses the idea of ‘ return to an earlier situation’ 3 cases    when     back      is  not   used to sell it again?

If we buy this house and then have to move somewhere else, how easy will it be The bike you sold me is too small. Can I sell it back to you?

Note the difference between sell back (to the same person) and sell again.

If I write to you, will you write back?

- I don't think he got your email. You'd better write again.

She comes to our parties but she never invites us back.

- Sam was really bad-mannered. I'm never going to invite him  again.
